results.title: GitHub - runtypelabs/persona: Create agentic front-end experiences ...
results.content:
## Latest commit

## History

## Repository files navigation

Persona.js

Persona.js

npm
Live demo
Ask DeepWiki

npm
Live demo
Ask DeepWiki

A themeable, pluggable AI chat widget for websites: built in TypeScript with zero framework dependencies. It renders using Vanilla JS. Its initial bundle tries hard to be small.

Persona gives you a drop-in UI for your AI assistant that works on basically any site or product on the web. It ships with support for streaming responses, direct client-token installs, WebMCP/page tools, built-in local client tools, voice I/O, multi-modal content, tool call visualization, approval gates, artifact rendering, safe markdown/HTML rendering, and a plugin system so you can customize every layer of the UI.

Persona chat widget slideshow [...] `examples/ai-sdk-webmcp`

## Bring Your Own Backend

Persona is designed to be backend-agnostic. You can plug it into any streaming agent or model SDK using the Persona SSE protocol. [...] ## Extending & contributing

Built a plugin, theme, backend adapter, or product with Persona? See the
extensibility map (EXTENDING.md) and
CONTRIBUTING.md. While Persona is launching, please
contribute customizations back to this monorepo as demos, examples, docs, or
package changes so the ecosystem grows in one place.

## Proxy Deployment

Both proxy examples handle secure API key management, CORS, and multiple flow configurations.

`pnpm dev`
`api/`
`pnpm dev:runtype-workers`

## Publishing

This monorepo uses Changesets for version management.

See `packages/widget/README.md` for the full configuration reference.

results.title: a vanilla-JS agent UI library with native WebMCP (MIT) | Hacker News
results.content: | |  |  | Show HN: Persona.js ‚Äì a vanilla-JS agent UI library with native WebMCP (MIT) (persona-chat.dev) | | 7 points by becomevocal 2 hours ago | hide | past | favorite | 11 comments | | Hey everyone. My cofounder and I are formally open sourcing (MIT) persona.js. It's a VanillaJS library that helps anyone build agentic experiences on the web, without a framework dependency, and full WebMCP support So, why'd we do this?  1) We're super fans of the web and the browser can do a ton today  2) We've seen AI builds be way overly complex because the FE requires a large project within an existing app OR the site wasn't using a framework to begin with  If you've been a part of huge apps with multiple frameworks inside them, or work inside CMS / website builders / ecomm platforms... you know [...] reply | | | |  |  | bookernath 2 hours ago  | parent | next (javascript:void(0))   It's definitely early - it shipped in the very latest version of chrome just this week, and the spec may still evolve (as a fair warning to early adopters). You can turn it on for your site via the chrome origin trial, or just install the polyfill.  Our use case with Persona is all about extending existing apps to have "AI assistance" that actually works well, and Persona being the AI assistant you can ship yourself in your site to enhance your app. There's a parallel camp who are interested in adding WebMCP tools to their sites so they can be used effectively by future browser-level agents (e.g. Gemini in Chrome), but that's not a thing today.  Some AI agents can already do best-effort operation of [...] inside CMS / website builders / ecomm platforms... you know what we're talking about. A "simple" AI feature disrupts your life for months  If you have a singular React codebase and can't possibly imagine building an interface without JSX, nothing to see here! You already have a lot of great options that are really cool too. Check out Assistant UI / CopilotKit / AI Elements which are all MIT  Some specifics:  Persona has a ~15 kB brotli to first paint (the full widget lazy-loads on first click) while being able to render most of the primary agent experiences you see on the web: from 'Fin' (pill launcher) to 'Claude' (fullscreen assistant) to 'Shopify Sidekick' (docked)  You can run it in Shadow DOM isolated mode so existing styles play nice  Everything has hooks and events, so you can add

results.title: Persona AI Agent UI Framework with WebMCP Support | Nathan Booker posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content:
We just shipped a new version of Persona; it‚Äôs now the first AI agent UI framework with native WebMCP support. WebMCP is still early, but the idea is simple: a web app can expose its routes, state, and actions to an agent as structured tools. Instead of making the agent scrape the DOM and guess what‚Äôs possible, the product can tell it what it can actually do.
That changes what ‚Äúchat in your app‚Äù can feel like. With WebMCP, your app can define where the agent can navigate, what actions are available on each page, what requires approval, and how those actions should appear in the UI. Alex Nahas from MCP-B had a framing I liked: a top-level navigation tool can work almost like the ‚Äúsystem prompt‚Äù for a site; the agent learns the shape of the product first, then acts in context. [...] Add a streaming AI chat to any website, in minutes.

Pete Fernando, graphic

Moving away from DOM scraping makes these agents so much more reliable.

results.title: NEW Browser API That Makes AI Agents Actually Work (WebMCP)
results.content: while I'm on this site here, saying that I want to book a round trip flight for two people from London to New York on specific dates, and I hit send, it is going to take me to the search result page. So, it has used the website for me. crazy stuff, right? Yeah, as I said, this demo was going to look very basic, but the key thing about Web MCP is how it used that site for me. The current approach to AI using websites tends to be using tools like Playwright, HTML parsing, or even taking screenshots of your site and trying to use it as a human, but all of that is pretty inefficient, especially token-wise, and it's still prone to a lot of errors. So, this is what Web MCP is here to fix. Web MCP instead lets the developer of the website expose certain MCP tools that then interact with the [...] There's a new proposal backed by Google and Microsoft that could be shaping the future of how we use the web, and I kind of like it. It's called Web MCP, but don't get that confused for any normal MCP server. Instead, Web MCP is actually a browser API, and it will let front-end developers expose features of their sites as tools to AI agents. Essentially, letting every site become a mini MCP server. And while you may have already seen some sites start to offer their own MCP servers already, this is a little bit different. Its goal is to actually help agents use the website for you instead of just accessing your APIs and showing that in a chat. It'll be entirely front-end based. Now, if that distinction does sound a little confusing, let's just jump in and see a demo and talk about why I [...] need to know what the website looks like at all, or what the HTML looks like, either. It simply knows it has that Web MCP tool, and it calls it with those input arguments. Then, I, the developer, have set what happens when I take in those input arguments, and I run a JavaScript function, which in this case simply updates the React state, and that causes a navigation to the search flight page. If we take a look at the front-end code for this, it is incredibly simple, and hopefully it will start to make a lot more sense. You can see the first thing we need to do is register the Web MCP tools that are available for a given page. And we can do that by using window.navigator.modelContext. So, this is the API that's going to need to be built into the browsers if this proposal passes, and it's
